message.content:
Test file: src/cli.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/cli.ts
Passing tests: 48 (all CLI + domain tests passing, no todo).
Recent changes: Added main() entry point and two process-level integration tests (happy path + error path).

This is the final refactor pass for the whole project. Review src/cli.ts and src/claim-office.ts for any final clarity/duplication/typing improvements while keeping all 48 tests green AND keeping `npx tsc --noEmit` clean (run it to confirm). Pay attention to: the `scenario: any` type in runScenario could be given a proper type for clarity — consider adding a Scenario interface if it improves intent without breaking tests. Only make changes that keep all tests green and tsc clean. Read both files first.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, proceed to next test after completion.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

> vitest run

content.text:
All 48 tests pass. The `tsc --noEmit` errors are entirely pre-existing: they come from missing `@types/node` (affecting `node:fs`, `node:url`, `node:child_process`, `process`), which is unrelated to the `scenario: any` typing. None of those errors touch the code I'd be refactoring, and installing `@types/node` is outside the scope of "only make changes that keep tests green and tsc clean."

The key refactor target is `runScenario(scenario: any)`. I'll add a `Scenario` interface to reveal intent and remove the `any`. Let me verify the exact shapes against the tests and implementation.

The data shapes used:
- `scenario.customer.yearsWithMHPCO` (number)
- `scenario.steps` — array of either:
  - quote step: `{ op: "quote", items: { type: string; material?; cursed?; enchantment? }[] }`
  - claim step: `{ op: "claim", policy: number, incident: { cause: string; damages: { itemType: string; amount: number }[] } }`

The `items` in a quote step match `QuoteInput["items"]`, and `incident` matches `ClaimInput["incident"]`. I'll reuse those types to avoid duplication (Rule 3) and reveal intent (Rule 2).

## Naming Evaluation (First Priority)

- `runScenario` — clearly reveals intent: runs a scenario of steps. Keep.
- `scenario: any` — the `any` hides all structure. A `Scenario` interface reveals intent. **This is the change to make.**

## Initial APP Mass (signature/typing only)

The `any` parameter contributes effectively nothing measurable to mass, but it costs clarity (Rule 2) and type safety. The refactor improves Rule 2 at zero behavioral cost.

Now let me design the interface. I'll reuse `QuoteInput["items"]` and `ClaimInput["incident"]` from `claim-office.ts` to avoid duplicating the item/incident shapes.

content.content: The file /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-29_12-05-52_claim-office-example-mapping_v4-exact-subagents_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/cli.ts has been updated successfully. (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

content.text: Now update the `runScenario` signature to use `Scenario`. The discriminated union on `op` will let TypeScript narrow each step, so `step.items`, `step.policy`, and `step.incident` are accessed safely within their branches.

input.old_string: export function runScenario(scenario: any): { results: unknown[] } {
input.new_string: export function runScenario(scenario: Scenario): { results: unknown[] } {

content.content: The file /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-29_12-05-52_claim-office-example-mapping_v4-exact-subagents_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/cli.ts has been updated successfully. (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

content.text:
The discriminated union narrows `step` to `ClaimStep` in the `else if (step.op === "claim")` branch, so `step.policy` and `step.incident` are typed. But `scenario.steps[step.policy]` returns a `Step` (union), and `policyStep.items` only exists on `QuoteStep`. Let me check that this still type-checks. The `main()` function parses untyped JSON — `JSON.parse` returns `any`, which is assignable to `Scenario`, so that stays clean.

Let me verify with the project's actual type-checking path. Since `npx tsc --noEmit` fails on missing `@types/node` regardless, I need to isolate whether my changes introduce *new* errors beyond the pre-existing node-types ones. Let me run tsc and compare.

content.text:
My change introduced two NEW errors at lines 90 and 92: `policyStep.items` — because `scenario.steps[step.policy]` is typed as the union `Step`, and `items` only exists on `QuoteStep`. A claim's `policy` index always points to a quote step (by the data contract), but TypeScript can't know that. I need to narrow it.

The cleanest, intent-revealing fix is to assert the referenced step is a quote step. Let me look at the current claim branch.

I'll add a guard that the referenced step is a quote step. This both reveals intent (a claim must reference a quote policy) and narrows the type. This is a clarity improvement (Rule 2) and adds a meaningful runtime assertion consistent with the other `assert*` guards in the file.

content.text: After the guard, `policyStep` narrows to `QuoteStep`, so `.items` is valid. Let me re-run tsc.

To make tsc genuinely clean as requested, the proper fix is installing `@types/node` — but that modifies dependencies, which is broader than the refactor scope and the task says "only make changes that keep tests green and tsc clean." The pre-existing errors are environmental (missing dev type package), not a code-clarity problem. Let me check whether `@types/node` is already available in the pnpm store so a one-line tsconfig/types change could resolve it without a network install.

input.command: find / -maxdepth 8 -type d -name node -path "*@types*" 2>/dev/null | head; echo "---"; ls node_modules/.pnpm 2>/dev/null | grep -i "types+node" | head

content.text:
`@types/node` is not present anywhere in the environment, so I cannot make those pre-existing errors disappear without a network install (out of scope and not part of this refactor). 

The important verification: my refactor introduces **zero new tsc errors**. The baseline (before my edits) had exactly these same `@types/node` errors, and after my edits the set of errors is identical minus my temporary intermediate ones — confirming the `Scenario` typing is sound. Now let me confirm all 48 tests stay green.

content.text: The refactored code reads cleanly. The `import` lines can be consolidated — `quote, claim` (values) and `QuoteInput, ClaimInput` (types) both come from `./claim-office.js`. I can merge them into a single import using inline `type` modifiers, removing duplication (Rule 3) and reducing elements (Rule 4).

import type { QuoteInput, ClaimInput } from "./claim-office.js";

content.text:
Refactor Phase Complete (final pass)

Naming Evaluation (first priority)
- `runScenario` — already reveals intent (runs a scenario's steps). Kept.
- `scenario: any` — the `any` hid all structure. Replaced with a named `Scenario` type. This was the primary clarity win the task flagged.

Refactorings applied (all in `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-29_12-05-52_claim-office-example-mapping_v4-exact-subagents_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/cli.ts`)
1. Rule 2 (Reveals Intent): Added a `Scenario` type built from a discriminated union of `QuoteStep | ClaimStep` on the `op` field. Reused `QuoteInput["items"]` and `ClaimInput["incident"]` from `claim-office.ts` instead of re-declaring those shapes (Rule 3, no duplication). `runScenario(scenario: any)` is now `runScenario(scenario: Scenario)`.
2. Rule 2 + type-safety: The discriminated union surfaced a latent invariant — a claim's `policy` index must point at a quote step. Added a guard `if (policyStep?.op !== "quote") throw new Error(...)` that both narrows `policyStep` to `QuoteStep` (so `.items` type-checks) and documents the contract at runtime, consistent with the existing `assert*` guards. The intermediate tsc errors at `policyStep.items` were resolved by this narrowing.
3. Rule 3/4 (no duplication / fewest elements): Consolidated two separate imports from `./claim-office.js` into one statement using inline `type` modifiers.

APP / mass note: This is a type-and-clarity refactor; the value-level mass of `runScenario` is essentially unchanged. The guard adds one conditional (mass +4) but buys both type narrowing and an explicit invariant — a justified Rule 2 trade per "clarity trumps APP."

claim-office.ts review
- Reviewed in full. Already clean: named constants, focused helpers (`hasHighEnchantment`, `reimbursementBeforeDeductible`, `payoutCap`), and clear rounding/cap comments. The per-type counting logic resembles `cli.ts`'s `countByType`, but extracting a shared helper would couple the domain module to the CLI across different element shapes (string[] vs item[]) for marginal gain — that would hurt Rule 4 and architecture boundaries, so no change was made. Documented as intentionally not refactored.

Verification
- Tests: 48/48 passing (36 domain + 12 CLI).
- `npx tsc --noEmit`: The refactor introduces zero new errors. The remaining errors are pre-existing and environmental — `@types/node` is not installed (confirmed absent from the pnpm store), so `node:fs`, `node:url`, `node:child_process`, and `process` are unresolved. These existed identically at baseline before any edit. Installing `@types/node` would modify dependencies, which is outside the stated refactor scope; the existing `tsx`/vitest toolchain runs the code fine regardless.

Wrote `experiment-done.txt` containing `DONE` per experiment mode.

Relevant files:
- `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-29_12-05-52_claim-office-example-mapping_v4-exact-subagents_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/cli.ts`
- `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-29_12-05-52_claim-office-example-mapping_v4-exact-subagents_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts`
